Account Profile: Acme Corp (ACME-001). Healthy enterprise account with stable usage across all dimensions. All data sources available and returning clean data. Green health score history. No anomalies or risk signals.
Purpose: Verify the basic end-to-end flow works correctly. All data sources return clean data, the agent produces accurate analysis with a Green health score, and the final report is at professional standard with no major risks flagged.
Input: account_id: ACME-001, Q4 data. Healthy account: stable usage (including a 15% usage increase this quarter), no open P1 tickets, Green health score history. All APIs (Salesforce, Zendesk, Mixpanel, Slack) returning normally.
Expected Behaviour: Agent gathers all data cleanly from Salesforce, Zendesk, Mixpanel, and Slack. Produces a Green health score with correct justification. Flags no major risks. Generates a professional report comparable to a manually written version, with minor or no edits needed at HIL checkpoints.
Actual Behaviour: Agent gathered all data successfully from all four sources. Produced a Green health score with correct justification citing stable usage, no P1 tickets, and positive sentiment. Report quality was comparable to the manually written version. Zero changes made at hil_review_analysis; one minor wording tweak at hil_review_report (phrasing preference, not a correction).
Verdict Rationale: All data retrieved correctly, health score matched manual assessment (Green), risk flags were appropriate (none), and report was at professional standard. The only human edit was a minor wording preference at the report review checkpoint — not a correction of substance.
Issues Found: Exec summary was slightly generic ("account remains healthy") — a manually written summary would have led with the 15% usage increase as the headline. Minor quality gap in narrative framing, not a correctness issue.
Notes: Minor quality gap in exec summary — the agent produced a correct but generic lead rather than highlighting the most newsworthy metric (15% usage increase). Consider adding prompt guidance to identify the single most significant data point for the headline. Not severe enough to warrant a fix in this iteration cycle.

Test Case Name: At-Risk Account — Declining with Upcoming Renewal
Account Profile: Globex Inc (GLOBEX-042). Enterprise account with declining health signals: usage down 30% QoQ, 3 open P1 tickets, renewal in 60 days, negative sentiment in Slack channels including frustration from the VP of Engineering — a key decision-maker.
Purpose: Verify the agent correctly identifies and escalates risk. Expects an Amber or Red health score, churn risk flagged with specific evidence (usage decline, P1 tickets, negative executive sentiment), and the 60-day renewal timeline surfaced as a risk amplifier.
Input: account_id: GLOBEX-042, Q4 data. Usage down 30% QoQ, 3 open P1 tickets, renewal in 60 days, negative Slack sentiment including clearly frustrated messages from the VP of Engineering alongside general end-user complaints.
Expected Behaviour: Agent flags Red or Amber health score. Identifies churn risk with specific evidence (usage decline, P1 tickets, negative sentiment). Surfaces the 60-day renewal timeline as a risk amplifier. Sentiment analysis recognises that executive-level frustration represents higher risk than end-user complaints. Report recommends urgent action with specifics.
Actual Behaviour: Agent correctly scored Red, identified churn risk, and cited the usage decline and open P1s as evidence. However, sentiment analysis scored "Needs Attention" instead of "Critical" — the Slack threads contained clearly frustrated messages from the VP of Engineering, but the SENTIMENT_ANALYSIS_PROMPT treated all messages with equal weight regardless of stakeholder seniority. The report did not distinguish between end-user grumbles and executive-level frustration.
Verdict Rationale: Health score and churn risk identification were correct (Red, with usage and ticket evidence), but sentiment severity was underrated. The SENTIMENT_ANALYSIS_PROMPT treats all Slack messages equally — a frustrated VP and a frustrated junior user produce the same signal. For this account the other signals (usage decline, P1 tickets) were strong enough to produce the correct Red score, but for a borderline account this gap would produce the wrong verdict. Partial because the overall conclusion was correct but the reasoning path had a material gap.
Issues Found: Sentiment analysis does not weight by stakeholder seniority or role. VP of Engineering frustration near a renewal is a materially higher risk signal than end-user complaints, but the prompt has no instruction to differentiate. The report's sentiment section rated "Needs Attention" when the manually written report rated "Critical" for the same data.
Notes: Root cause is in SENTIMENT_ANALYSIS_PROMPT — it analyses Slack thread sentiment but gives no guidance on weighting by stakeholder role or seniority. Fixed in Iteration 1 by adding stakeholder weighting instructions. After fix, TC2 re-tested as Pass with sentiment correctly scored "Critical" citing the VP of Engineering's messages as the primary signal.

Account Profile: Initech Ltd (INITECH-007). Standard account with one data source failure: Slack API configured to return an error (simulated outage). All other data sources (Salesforce, Zendesk, Mixpanel) functioning normally. Tests the agent's graceful degradation behaviour.
Purpose: Verify graceful degradation when a data source fails. The agent should detect the error, flag the missing data explicitly in the report (not silently skip it or hallucinate), produce analysis from remaining sources, and not crash or produce corrupted output.
Input: account_id: INITECH-007, Q4 data. Slack API configured to return an error (simulated outage). Salesforce, Zendesk, and Mixpanel APIs functioning normally. Account otherwise has a typical health profile.
Expected Behaviour: Agent detects the Slack API error and records sentiment as "Unknown" or equivalent. Flags the gap explicitly in the report with a clear statement like "Sentiment data was unavailable." Produces analysis from remaining sources (tickets, usage) without the sentiment dimension. Does not hallucinate sentiment data or silently omit the section. Health score is based on available data only, with an explicit caveat about the missing dimension.
Actual Behaviour: Agent correctly detected the Slack error, recorded score: "Unknown" for sentiment, and flagged the gap in the report. The synthesis node handled the missing dimension without crashing — health score was based on tickets and usage only, with an explicit caveat. Report included the line "Sentiment data was unavailable for this period" in the Sentiment section. No silent omission, no hallucinated data.
Verdict Rationale: The agent handled the API failure exactly as designed: explicit error detection, transparent reporting of the gap ("Sentiment data was unavailable for this period"), no hallucination, and graceful degradation to analysis using available data sources only. The health score was computed from tickets and usage with an appropriate caveat. This is the target behaviour for data source failures.
Issues Found: None — this is the target behaviour for data source failures. The error handling path built in Stage 5 worked as designed.
Notes: The explicit "Sentiment data was unavailable for this period" line in the report is exactly right — transparent about what is missing without over-qualifying the rest of the analysis. The error handling in the fetch_slack_threads tool correctly caught the API error and returned an empty result with an error flag rather than propagating the exception.

Account Profile: NovaTech (NOVA-015). Newly onboarded account with only 3 weeks of usage data since onboarding. 2 open P4 tickets (setup questions, not complaints). No Slack channel history established yet. Limited Salesforce records. No prior quarter available for trend comparison. Tests whether the agent handles data sparsity without over-interpreting.
Purpose: Verify the agent does not over-interpret sparse data. With only 3 weeks of usage data, no prior quarter for trend comparison, and minimal interaction history, the agent should not produce a confident health score or extrapolate trends. It should note data limitations explicitly and recommend reassessment after a full quarter.
Input: account_id: NOVA-015, Q4 data. New account onboarded 3 weeks ago. 2 open P4 tickets (setup and configuration questions). 3 weeks of usage data with no prior quarter for trend comparison. No Slack channel established yet. Limited Salesforce records (contract signed, onboarding kickoff logged).
Expected Behaviour: Agent recognises insufficient data for a confident assessment. Produces health score with an "Insufficient Data" or "Too Early" qualifier rather than a definitive Green/Amber/Red. Does not extrapolate trends from 3 weeks of data. Report includes explicit caveats about data sufficiency and recommends reassessment after a full quarter of data has accumulated.
Actual Behaviour: Agent correctly identified the limited data period. Usage analysis noted that trend calculations require at least one prior quarter and were not applicable. Sentiment was marked as "No Data" (no Slack channel). Ticket analysis correctly characterised the 2 P4 tickets as onboarding-related, not indicative of health issues. Health score was produced with an "Insufficient Data" qualifier. Report included appropriate caveats about the 3-week data window and recommended a full reassessment at end of the next full quarter.
Verdict Rationale: The agent appropriately handled sparse data without over-interpreting. It did not extrapolate trends from insufficient data, clearly flagged data limitations across multiple dimensions (usage trends, sentiment, engagement patterns), and provided actionable next steps (reassess after a full quarter). The "Insufficient Data" qualifier on the health score is the correct output — a definitive score from 3 weeks of data would be misleading.
Issues Found: None — the agent correctly identified data limitations and adjusted its confidence level accordingly. The "Insufficient Data" qualifier is the right output for this scenario.
Notes: The agent handled sparse data well. Minor tweaks at both checkpoints were wording preferences (e.g., adjusting how the onboarding status was described), not corrections of substance. The recommendation to reassess after a full quarter was a useful addition that matches what the manual process also produces for new accounts. No regression from this test case after either iteration cycle.

Account Profile: Nexus Corp (NEXUS-200). Enterprise tier with two licensed products: Platform API and Analytics Dashboard. Platform API usage is strong (48% DAU/MAU, 72% feature adoption, 18K API calls/month — all above enterprise benchmarks). Analytics Dashboard usage is declining (15% DAU/MAU, 28% feature adoption, down 22% QoQ — well below benchmarks). 2 open P3 tickets on Analytics Dashboard. Sentiment split: engineering champion (Platform API power user) positive in Slack, business analysts (Analytics Dashboard users) frustrated about missing features and slow query performance. Renewal in 120 days.
Purpose: Verify the agent handles nuance and multi-product complexity without oversimplifying. The agent should surface per-product divergence — Platform API healthy, Analytics Dashboard critical — rather than blending metrics into a single score that masks the problem. It should recommend product-specific actions and anchor the health score justification to the specific product risk.
Input: account_id: NEXUS-200, Q4 data. Enterprise tier, two licensed products: Platform API (48% DAU/MAU, 72% feature adoption, 18K API calls/month — above enterprise benchmarks) and Analytics Dashboard (15% DAU/MAU, 28% feature adoption, down 22% QoQ — below benchmarks). 2 open P3 tickets on Analytics Dashboard, no P1s. Sentiment split: engineering champion positive (Platform API), business analysts frustrated (Analytics Dashboard — missing features, slow query performance). Renewal in 120 days.
Expected Behaviour: Agent surfaces per-product divergence — Platform API scored Healthy, Analytics Dashboard scored Critical. Report has separate product sections with per-product metrics and assessments. Recommends product-specific actions (immediate Analytics Dashboard adoption intervention with timeline before 120-day renewal). Health score Amber, with justification anchored to the Analytics Dashboard decline rather than a blended average. Synthesis cites product-specific evidence for risk assessment.
Actual Behaviour: Agent produced a single usage analysis with score "Needs Attention" and trend "Declining." Details mentioned "mixed adoption signals" but did not identify which product was declining or by how much. Platform API's strong metrics pulled blended averages up (31.5% DAU/MAU, 50% feature adoption), masking the severity of Analytics Dashboard's 15% DAU/MAU and 28% adoption. Synthesis scored Amber (coincidentally correct) but cited "moderate usage concerns" rather than product-specific risk. Report's Product Engagement section contained one paragraph blending both products together. Recommended actions were generic: "monitor usage trends and schedule a check-in."
Verdict Rationale: The agent cannot represent per-product divergence because the state schema has one usage_analysis field — this is a structural limitation, not an analysis error. The USAGE_ANALYSIS_PROMPT analysed the data it received and produced a reasonable single-score assessment given the combined input. The problem is that Scope Step 7 describes a single "Assess product engagement" step with no per-product dimension. The blended report would not trigger the needed action — a CSM reading "Needs Attention" with generic recommendations would miss the critical Analytics Dashboard decline that requires immediate intervention.
Issues Found: State schema has no per-product analysis field (one usage_analysis dict for all products). Scope Step 7 assumes single-product accounts. analyse_health node makes one usage LLM call with all metrics combined. Blended averages (31.5% DAU/MAU, 50% adoption) mask per-product divergence. Generic recommendations ("monitor usage trends") instead of product-specific actions ("immediate Analytics Dashboard adoption intervention"). Report lacks per-product sections — one blended paragraph instead.
Notes: Root cause traced through systematic elimination of four layers: Tool ✓ (fetch_usage_metrics returned correct data for both products), Prompt ✓ (USAGE_ANALYSIS_PROMPT produced a reasonable single-score assessment given the combined data it received), Graph — partial (analyse_health makes one usage LLM call, but this is a consequence of the deeper problem), Scope ✗ (Step 7 has no per-product dimension). Fixed in Iteration 2 via cross-stage cascade: updated Scope Step 7 (per-product output and decision logic), Design state schema (added usage_analysis_by_product), Build analyse_health node (per-product loop + rollup) and prompts (USAGE_ANALYSIS_PROMPT, new USAGE_ROLLUP_PROMPT, SYNTHESIS_PROMPT). After fix, TC5 re-tested as Pass with separate product sections and product-specific recommendations.
